game_name: Wizardry: Proving Grounds of the Mad Overlord

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Basic class roles introduced; four elite classes (Bishop, Samurai, Lord, Ninja) unlocked after sufficient character progression in dungeon
Basic equipment provided before first descent; treasure hunting drives equipment progression throughout dungeon
20x20 grid maze requires manual mapping on graph paper; navigation challenges escalate with each level
Combat groups of 1-4 monsters; difficulty scales with dungeon depth
Spells unlock gradually as characters advance; spell power increases with character level
Permanent darkness on certain squares forces blind movement; map documentation becomes critical
Player must manually enter coordinates; landing in solid stone or traps ends game permanently
Determines current party location; counters disorientation from darkness and teleport traps

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Higher character levels (exact dungeon level undocumented)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Late-game mobility spell; high risk/reward for rapid dungeon traversal
Ever-increasing item potency; primary progression driver alongside experience gains
Final boss encounter; amulet retrieval completes game objective

typical_relief_triggers: Returning to town (exit dungeon to save)
Using Location Detection spell to reorient
typical_tension_triggers: Risk of total party kill without save-inside-dungeon
Threat of permanent character death (bodies recoverable by new party)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Methodical exploration with escalating danger; tension mitigated by mandatory town returns for saves. Difficulty compounds across 10-level progression with no mid-dungeon checkpoints, creating long, high-stakes exploration cycles. Hundreds of hours to completion indicates sustained, extended pacing.

spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Darkness spell squares and teleportation trap squares marked permanently on dungeon squares; manual map documentation required to avoid becoming lost

difficulty_curve.early_game: Moderate baseline; players learn navigation mechanics, basic combat, and mapping on levels 1-3. Character creation and equipment provide foundation. Darkness and teleport hazards introduced gradually.
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Escalating complexity on levels 4-7; monster encounters increase in difficulty and number. Spell system mastery becomes critical. Navigation hazards (darkness, teleports) require experienced map management. Risk of disorientation compounds.
difficulty_curve.late_game: Severe difficulty on levels 8-10; boss-level monsters and coordinated enemy groups. Teleportation spell becomes viable option for progression. Final encounter with Werdna arch-wizard on level 10.
notable_spikes: Introduction of darkness squares (blind navigation) - mid-early game
Transition to elite classes unlocking (mid-to-late) - game-changing progression spike
Werdna encounter on level 10 - final difficulty wall

notable_level_patterns.description: Each of 10 levels presents increasingly complex 20x20 dungeon maze. Difficulty tied to dungeon depth; escalation forces continuous tactical adaptation and map mastery.
Spatial hazards (darkness squares, teleport traps) introduced sequentially across levels rather than all at start. Allows player skill development before maximum chaos.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Darkness introduced early-to-mid; teleportation hazards mid-to-late

notable_level_patterns.description: Mandatory save points in town create risk/reward cycles. Players must exit dungeon to save progress; deep runs risk total party wipe and unsaved losses. Extends psychological tension across multiple game sessions.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Applies to all levels; no in-dungeon saves

notable_level_patterns.description: Detection and teleportation spells unlock at higher character levels, creating emergent solutions to navigation hazards. Early-game navigation forced to manual mapping; late-game allows magical shortcuts.
